name: UX Analytics & Metrics
description: Comprehensive guide to measuring user experience through analytics, metrics, and data-driven decision making

why_measure_ux.quote: In God we trust. All others must bring data. - W. Edwards Deming

behavioral.use_when: Understanding actual behavior (more reliable than stated)

types_of_metrics.recommendation: Combine quantitative + qualitative, and attitudinal + behavioral for complete picture

task_success_rate.definition: % of users who complete a task successfully
task_success_rate.measurement: Usability testing or analytics (track goal completions)

time_on_task.definition: How long it takes to complete a task
time_on_task.measurement: Usability testing (stopwatch) or analytics (time between events)
considerations: High variance? Task unclear or multiple paths
time_on_task.note: Long time may indicate confusion OR engagement (depends on task)

error_rate.definition: Number of errors per task attempt
error_rate.measurement: Usability testing (observe errors) or analytics (track failed attempts)

sus.name: System Usability Scale
sample_questions: I think that I would like to use this system frequently
I found the system unnecessarily complex
I thought the system was easy to use

nps.name: Net Promoter Score
nps.question: How likely are you to recommend [product] to a friend or colleague? (0-10)
nps.follow_up: Ask 'Why did you give that score?' (qualitative insight)

csat.name: Customer Satisfaction Score
csat.question: How satisfied are you with [product/feature]?
csat.calculation: % who selected 4 or 5 (Satisfied or Very Satisfied)
csat.when: After specific interaction (purchase, support ticket, feature use)

ces.name: Customer Effort Score
ces.question: How easy was it to [complete task]?
ces.finding: Effort is more predictive of loyalty than satisfaction (CEB research)

dau.name: Daily Active Users
dau.definition: Users who interact with product daily

wau.name: Weekly Active Users
wau.definition: Users who interact with product weekly

mau.name: Monthly Active Users
mau.definition: Users who interact with product monthly

session_duration.definition: Time user spends in a session
session_duration.interpretation: Longer may indicate engagement OR confusion (depends on product)
session_duration.use_case: Content sites (longer good), task-based apps (shorter may be good)

pages_per_session.definition: Number of pages viewed per session
pages_per_session.interpretation: Higher = more engagement (content sites) OR poor IA (task sites)

bounce_rate.definition: % of sessions where user views only one page and leaves

adoption_rate.definition: % of users who've used a feature
adoption_rate.formula: Users who used feature / Total users × 100
adoption_rate.goal: High for core features, lower OK for advanced features

frequency_of_use.definition: How often users engage with feature
frequency_of_use.measurement: Average uses per user per time period

depth_of_use.example: % of users who use advanced settings vs basic

conversion_rate.definition: % of users who complete desired action (purchase, sign-up, etc.)
conversion_rate.micro_conversions: Smaller goals leading to main goal (add to cart, start checkout)

drop_off_rate.definition: % who leave at each funnel step
drop_off_rate.formula: Users who didn't proceed / Users who reached step × 100

funnel_metrics.funnel_visualization: Visualize drop-off at each step (Analytics → Funnel report)

ltv.name: Lifetime Value
ltv.definition: Average revenue per customer over their lifetime

cac.name: Customer Acquisition Cost
cac.formula: Total marketing + sales cost / New customers

page_load_time.definition: Time for page to fully load
page_load_time.impact: 1 second delay = 7% reduction in conversions (Akamai study)

lcp.name: Largest Contentful Paint
lcp.definition: Time for largest element to render

fid.name: First Input Delay
fid.definition: Time from first interaction to browser response

time_to_interactive.definition: Time until page is fully interactive

pros: No code changes for new events
use_for: When you don't know what to track yet
Budget option for heatmaps and recordings

types.click_heatmap: Where users click (find dead clicks, popular elements)
types.scroll_heatmap: How far users scroll (where to place CTAs)
types.move_heatmap: Mouse movement (attention, though imperfect on mobile)

adoption.description: New users of a product or feature

segmentation.description: Break down metrics by user groups

segments.behavioral: Power users, casual users, new users
segments.temporal: Time of day, day of week

segmentation.example: Overall conversion 5%, but mobile 2%, desktop 8% (mobile issue!)

cohort_analysis.description: Group users by shared characteristic (e.g., sign-up date)
use_cases: Retention over time (Do users from Jan retain better than Feb?)
Feature impact (Do users who adopted feature X retain better?)

funnel_analysis.example: E-commerce: View Product → Add to Cart → Checkout → Payment → Confirmation

statistical_significance.description: Determine if results are due to chance or real effect

p_value.definition: Probability result is due to chance
p_value.interpretation: p = 0.03 means 3% chance result is due to chance (significant!)

sample_size.calculator: Use A/B test sample size calculator
sample_size.rule_of_thumb: At least 30 users per variant (more for small effects)

duration.avoid: Stopping test early because winning (regression to mean)

principles.glanceability: Understand at a glance (no hunting for info)
principles.context: Show trends, comparisons, benchmarks (not just numbers)

elements.trends: Line charts showing change over time
elements.comparisons: Current vs previous period (%, absolute change)
elements.alerts: Highlight when metric is outside normal range

table.example: Top pages by traffic and conversion

tools.google_data_studio: Free, integrates with GA and other Google tools (now Looker Studio)

best_practices: Define goals before measuring (what do you want to improve?)
Combine quantitative and qualitative data (what + why)
Track both attitudinal and behavioral metrics
Use standardized surveys (SUS, NPS) for benchmarking
Segment data (don't rely on averages)
Ensure statistical significance (p < 0.05, sufficient sample size)
Run A/B tests long enough (1-4 weeks, not days)
Track Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS)
Use funnel analysis to find bottlenecks
Cohort analysis for retention insights
Set up dashboards for key metrics (glanceable)
Review metrics regularly (weekly or monthly)
Make data accessible to team (not siloed)
Focus on actionable metrics (not vanity metrics)
Validate insights with users (don't just trust data)
Use heatmaps and session recordings to understand behavior
Track both macro and micro conversions
Monitor performance metrics (speed impacts UX and conversion)
Test one variable at a time (A/B testing)
Document findings and decisions (build institutional knowledge)
anti_patterns: Ignoring qualitative data (numbers don't explain why)
Not sharing data with team (siloed insights)
Testing multiple variables at once (can't isolate cause)
Not tracking long-term impacts (optimize for short-term only)
Over-relying on one metric (need holistic view)
No action from insights (data without decisions is waste)
Ignoring outliers without investigation (may reveal issues)

obama_campaign_ab_testing.result: $60 million in additional donations from A/B testing

netflix.result: Focus shifted to content and retention

resources: Lean Analytics (Alistair Croll, Benjamin Yoskovitz) - Metrics for startups
Measuring the User Experience (Tullis & Albert) - UX metrics bible
Quantifying the User Experience (Sauro & Lewis) - Statistics for UX
Google Analytics Academy - Free GA training
HEART Framework (Google Research) - UX metrics framework
Nielsen Norman Group - UX measurement articles
A/B Testing Mastery (Peep Laja) - CXL course
